message:
fix(core): bump frankensearch pin and unify mux socket discovery (GH #46, #48)

- Bump frankensearch rev to 7086d964 (HEAD) which handles the
  LockError::PolledAfterCompletion variant from asupersync 0.2.9,
  unblocking the timer-fix upgrade (GH #46).

- Create shared `discover_mux_socket()` resolver in wezterm.rs that
  checks config path, WEZTERM_UNIX_SOCKET env var, AND canonical
  WezTerm default unix-domain paths (via vendored config crate).
  Previously upper layers (build_unified_client, detect_tailer_mode,
  detect_wezterm_socket) only checked config/env, missing live sockets
  at canonical paths like /run/user/UID/wezterm/sock (GH #48).

- Add `discover_canonical_mux_socket()` in vendored.rs to probe
  WezTerm config unix domains and default unix domains.

- Update all three socket discovery callers to use the shared resolver:
  build_unified_client, streaming_socket_configured, detect_wezterm_socket.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"ft-2oph2.2","title":"[ft-2oph2][perf] Reuse chunked scan buffer for definitive trigger scan","description":"# Goal
Eliminate duplicate full-buffer accumulation in the chunked scan pipeline when both trigger scanning and compression are enabled.

## Scope
- File: `crates/frankenterm-core/src/scan_pipeline.rs`
- Reuse the existing uncompressed chunk buffer as the definitive flush-time trigger input whenever compression is enabled, instead of also appending the same bytes into `trigger_data_buffer`.
- Preserve exact batch/chunk trigger parity and compression behavior.
- Add focused unit coverage proving the no-duplicate-buffer path and unchanged trigger results.

## Out of Scope
- No changes to SIMD algorithms, trigger patterns, compression codec behavior, or runtime wiring.
- No changes to unrelated runtime/event/storage files.

## Validation
- `rch exec -- cargo test -p frankenterm-core --lib scan_pipeline -- --nocapture`
- `rch exec -- cargo test -p frankenterm-core --test proptest_scan_pipeline -- --nocapture`
- `rch exec -- cargo bench -p frankenterm-core --bench scan_pipeline --no-run`

## Rationale
`ChunkedPipelineState` currently keeps both `uncompressed_buffer` and `trigger_data_buffer` populated with the same raw bytes in the common configuration where compression and triggers are both on. This inflates memory traffic and buffer growth on the hot path without changing behavior.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"TealLotus","created_at":"2026-03-31T18:02:43.603376Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-01T17:20:17.917970Z","closed_at":"2026-04-01T17:20:17.917717Z","close_reason":"Implemented chunked scan buffer reuse, strengthened compression-enabled trigger parity tests, and validated via rch-backed scan_pipeline/proptest/bench coverage; remaining repo-wide failures were unrelated.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ft-2oph2.2","depends_on_id":"ft-2oph2","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-31T18:02:43.603376Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"wa-1bznu","title":"Phase 4: Replace tokio task spawning with asupersync structured concurrency","description":"# Replace tokio task spawning with asupersync structured concurrency

## Goal
Replace all tokio::spawn/JoinHandle/JoinSet with asupersync's structured concurrency (Scope/region). This bead establishes the patterns; per-module application is done in module-specific beads.

## Key paradigm shift
Tokio allows detached tasks (fire-and-forget). Asupersync enforces structured concurrency — every task belongs to a Scope, and the scope waits for all children before exiting.

## Scope (what this bead covers)
- Establish cx.region() / scope.spawn() patterns
- Define how to replace JoinHandle (scope child handle)
- Define how to replace JoinSet (region with multiple spawns + semaphore for bounding)
- Document fire-and-forget redesign strategies:
  - Option A: Attach to long-lived application scope
  - Option B: Dedicated background region for app lifetime
  - Option C: Refactor to not need detached tasks
- Create helper utilities for common patterns (bounded spawn, spawn-with-timeout)

## What this does NOT cover
- select! macro replacement — that's per-module work because each module has different select! branches. Module beads (wa-p48pw, wa-1m7nk, wa-16hou, etc.) handle their own select! migration using asupersync::combinator::select/race.

## Patterns to establish
```rust
// Bounded concurrent work (replaces JoinSet with max)
pub async fn spawn_bounded<F, T>(
    cx: &mut Cx, max: usize, tasks: Vec<F>
) -> Outcome<Vec<T>, Error>
where F: FnOnce(&mut Cx) -> Fut...
{
    cx.region(|scope| async {
        let sem = Semaphore::new(max);
        for task in tasks {
            let permit = sem.acquire(cx).await?;
            scope.spawn(move |cx| async move {
                let _p = permit;
                task(cx).await
            });
        }
    }).await
}
```

## Unit tests
- Test: scope.spawn() runs task to completion
- Test: scope exit waits for all children
- Test: bounded spawn respects concurrency limit
- Test: cancellation propagates to all scope children
- Test: nested regions work correctly
- All tests use LabRuntime with seed-locked scheduling

## Acceptance criteria
- Structured concurrency patterns documented and tested
- Helper utilities for bounded spawn, spawn-with-timeout
- Fire-and-forget redesign strategy decided
- 5+ LabRuntime tests for the patterns

## LabRuntime DPOR
- **Structured concurrency testing with DPOR**: Use LabRuntime schedule exploration (DPOR) to exhaustively test structured concurrency guarantees:
  - Verify scope exit always waits for ALL children under every possible interleaving
  - Verify cancellation propagates to all scope children regardless of scheduling order
  - Verify nested region teardown ordering (inner scopes complete before outer)
  - Verify bounded spawn never exceeds concurrency limit under any schedule

## Benchmark requirements
- **Criterion benchmarks for task spawn overhead**: Add `benches/task_spawn.rs` measuring:
  - scope.spawn() latency for a no-op task
  - Region creation + N spawns + join overhead for N=1,10,100,1000
  - Comparison with tokio::spawn for equivalent workloads
  - Structured concurrency overhead: region vs flat spawn

## Cross-references
- See wa-brc7d.5 (mux crate in asupersync FrankenTerm crates epic) — the mux crate is the heaviest user of task spawning for pane output subscriptions and background polling.  {"id":"wa-1c2u","title":"Smart Scrollback with Importance Weighting","description":"## Goal
Replace WezTerm's fixed scrollback limit with importance-weighted scrollback that retains high-value output (errors, tool results, compilation output) longer than low-value output (blank lines, repeated prompts), reducing memory usage while preserving the content that matters.

## Background & Motivation
WezTerm's scrollback is a fixed-size ring buffer (default 3,500 lines per pane). With 50 panes, that's 175,000 lines of terminal cell data in memory. But scrollback content varies enormously in value:
- High value: error messages, compilation output, test results, tool_use boundaries
- Low value: blank lines, repeated prompts, ANSI cursor movements, progress bars

Fixed limits waste memory on low-value content while potentially evicting high-value content.

## Technical Design

### Importance Scoring
```rust
pub struct ScrollbackLine {
    pub cells: Vec<Cell>,
    pub importance: f64,  // 0.0 (garbage) to 1.0 (critical)
    pub timestamp: Instant,
}

fn score_line(line: &str, context: &PaneContext) -> f64 {
    let mut score = 0.3;  // baseline

    // High value indicators
    if patterns::is_error(line) { score += 0.4; }
    if patterns::is_tool_boundary(line) { score += 0.3; }
    if patterns::is_compilation_output(line) { score += 0.2; }
    if patterns::is_test_result(line) { score += 0.3; }

    // Low value indicators
    if line.trim().is_empty() { score -= 0.2; }
    if patterns::is_progress_bar(line) { score -= 0.3; }
    if patterns::is_ansi_only(line) { score -= 0.3; }

    score.clamp(0.0, 1.0)
}
```

### Eviction Policy
Instead of FIFO eviction, use importance-weighted eviction:
```rust
fn evict_line(scrollback: &mut VecDeque<ScrollbackLine>) -> Option<ScrollbackLine> {
    // Find lowest importance line in oldest 25% of scrollback
    let quarter = scrollback.len() / 4;
    let (min_idx, _) = scrollback.iter()
        .take(quarter)
        .enumerate()
        .min_by(|(_, a), (_, b)| a.importance.partial_cmp(&b.importance).unwrap())
        .unwrap();
    scrollback.remove(min_idx)
}
```

### Memory Budget
Instead of line count, use byte budget per pane:
```rust
pub struct ScrollbackConfig {
    pub byte_budget_per_pane: usize,  // default: 2MB
    pub min_lines: usize,             // always keep at least this many (500)
    pub max_lines: usize,             // hard cap (10,000)
    pub importance_threshold: f64,    // never evict above this (0.8)
}
```

### Integration with patterns.rs
Reuses existing pattern detection from patterns.rs (3-stage Bloom→Aho-Corasick→Regex) for line classification. The importance scorer is a thin wrapper over existing pattern rules.

### Implementation Location
- Modify: FrankenTerm fork's terminal/src/terminalstate/mod.rs (scrollback management)
- Integration: patterns.rs provides line classification
- Config: scrollback section in wa-core config

## Existing Code References
- patterns.rs: 100+ pattern rules, 3-stage matching (reuse for line classification)
- FrankenTerm terminal crate: scrollback ring buffer (modify eviction policy)

## Configuration
```toml
[scrollback]
mode = "importance_weighted"  # "fixed" | "importance_weighted"
byte_budget_per_pane_mb = 2
min_lines = 500
max_lines = 10000
importance_threshold = 0.8    # Never evict lines above this
```

## Dependencies
- FrankenTerm fork (bd-20fw): modifying terminal scrollback internals
- patterns.rs: reuse pattern rules for importance scoring
- Enhances memory reduction (wa-3kxe): less scrollback memory per pane

## Acceptance Criteria
- Importance scoring for each scrollback line
- Weighted eviction: low-value lines evicted before high-value
- Byte budget instead of line count
- Integration with existing pattern rules
- Configurable thresholds and budgets
- Unit tests: synthetic scrollback, verify high-value retention
- Memory benchmark: RSS reduction vs fixed scrollback under 50-pane load

## Benchmark Requirements
- **criterion benchmarks** with target `benches/importance_scoring.rs`:
  - `score_line_latency`: measure time to compute importance score for a single line. Target: <1us per line (must be fast enough for inline scoring during terminal output).
  - `score_batch_throughput`: measure scoring throughput for 10,000 lines of mixed content. Target: >1M lines/sec.
  - `eviction_selection`: measure time to find the lowest-importance line in the oldest 25% of a 10,000-line scrollback. Target: <50us per eviction decision.
  - `byte_budget_enforcement`: measure overhead of byte-budget tracking vs simple line-count tracking. Target: <5% overhead.

## Property-Based Testing (proptest)
- **Scoring monotonicity**: if line A matches strictly more high-value indicators than line B (and no more low-value indicators), then `score(A) >= score(B)`. More signals of importance always result in equal or higher scores.
- **Score range invariant**: for any input string, `score_line()` returns a value in [0.0, 1.0]. No combination of pattern matches can produce a score outside this range.
- **Eviction ordering**: lines with importance >= `importance_threshold` are never evicted while lines with importance < `importance_threshold` exist in the scrollback. The threshold is a hard floor.
- **Budget compliance**: after any sequence of insertions and evictions, `total_bytes <= byte_budget_per_pane` (within one line's tolerance for the last insertion that triggered eviction).

## Cross-References
- **wa-3r5e** (Scrollback memory pressure mitigation): wa-3r5e makes pane-level decisions about how aggressively to trim scrollback under memory pressure, while wa-1c2u makes line-level decisions about which lines to keep. Under pressure, wa-3r5e reduces the byte budget, and wa-1c2u uses importance scoring to decide which lines survive within that reduced budget.
- **wa-283h4.8** (Entropy-aware scheduling): entropy measurements can inform importance scoring — lines with high information entropy (novel content) should score higher than low-entropy lines (repeated patterns). This provides a principled, information-theoretic foundation for the heuristic scoring rules.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-09T22:45:14.755885Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-03-28T06:32:04.008025Z","closed_at":"2026-03-28T06:32:04.007797Z","close_reason":"Implemented: scrollback_eviction.rs (1980 lines) with importance-weighted eviction + voi.rs (value-of-information scoring)","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-1c2u","depends_on_id":"ft-20fw","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-09T23:16:56.597823Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"wa-1c2u","depends_on_id":"ft-ixt4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-09T23:16:56.597823Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"wa-1c2u","depends_on_id":"wa-3kxe","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-09T22:45:32.328834Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

## Goal
After all migration and testing is complete, remove tokio and ALL tokio-dependent crates.

## Removal steps
1. Remove from workspace Cargo.toml:
   - tokio
   - tokio-rustls
   - reqwest
2. Remove from wa-core/Cargo.toml:
   - Any direct tokio references
   - The asupersync-runtime feature flag (no longer needed)
   - All #[cfg(feature = "asupersync-runtime")] and #[cfg(not(feature = "asupersync-runtime"))] blocks
3. Remove the runtime_compat.rs module (served its purpose)
4. Remove any compatibility shims or adapter code

## Verification commands
```bash
# Must return zero matches
cargo tree 2>/dev/null | grep -ci tokio
# Must succeed
cargo build --all-features
cargo test --all-features
cargo clippy --all-features -- -D warnings
# Check for dead code
cargo +nightly udeps --all-targets  # if available
```

## Documentation updates
- Update AGENTS.md: replace tokio patterns with asupersync patterns
- Update any code comments referencing tokio
- Update README if it mentions tokio
- Add note about asupersync Cx pattern for new contributors

## Unit test for this bead
- Smoke test: build all targets with all features
- Regression test: full integration test suite still passes
- Lint: no warnings from clippy

## Acceptance criteria
- cargo tree shows ZERO tokio (direct or transitive)
- All features build and test cleanly
- No dead code from migration scaffolding
- Documentation updated
- clippy clean

## Cargo tree verification note
- **Automated verification**: After removing tokio, run `cargo tree -i tokio 2>&1` — this should fail with "package ID specification 'tokio' did not match any packages" (meaning tokio is completely absent). Also run `cargo tree --depth=100 | grep -i tokio` to catch any transitive dependency that might pull tokio back in. If any transitive dep still requires tokio (e.g., a third-party crate), it must be replaced or patched. Add a CI step that fails the build if tokio appears anywhere in `cargo tree` output.

## Cross-references
- See wa-1ms1 (update dependencies) — coordinate with the dependency update bead to ensure that no newly added or updated dependency reintroduces tokio as a transitive dependency.  {"id":"wa-2h5wv","title":"Refactor MuxPool (mux_pool.rs) to asupersync","description":"# Refactor MuxPool (mux_pool.rs) to asupersync

## Background
mux_pool.rs (583 LOC) wraps generic Pool for DirectMuxClient connections with WezTerm-specific health checks.

## Dependencies
- Pool refactoring (wa-36idf) must be done first
- DirectMuxClient refactoring (wa-p48pw) must be done first

## Migration
1. Thread Cx through to Pool methods
2. Update health check (list_panes()) to use Cx
3. Update connection factory (DirectMuxClient::connect) to use Cx
4. Ensure pool config works with Budget model

## Comprehensive unit tests (LabRuntime)
1. **Basic lifecycle**: create pool → acquire → use → release → shutdown
2. **Health check integration**: mock unhealthy client, verify pool replaces it
3. **Connection factory**: verify DirectMuxClient::connect called with correct Cx
4. **Idle eviction**: connections idle past timeout are evicted (virtual time)
5. **Concurrent acquire**: N tasks acquire simultaneously, max_size respected
6. **Acquire timeout**: full pool, Budget::deadline fires, clean Cancelled outcome
7. **Pool drain on shutdown**: scope exit waits for all connections returned
8. **Stats accuracy**: verify all counters under concurrent load

Each test logs: pool stats at each step, health check results, connection create/destroy events.

## Acceptance criteria
- MuxPool works with asupersync DirectMuxClient and Pool
- Health checks work through Cx
- Idle eviction works with virtual time
- 8+ LabRuntime tests
- Obligation leak oracle passes

## LabRuntime DPOR
- **Pool concurrency testing with DPOR**: Use LabRuntime schedule exploration (DPOR) to exhaustively test concurrent MuxPool operations:
  - Multiple tasks acquiring connections while health checks run — verify no stale connections returned
  - Interleaved acquire + release + eviction — verify pool invariants (active + idle <= max_size)
  - Concurrent shutdown while tasks hold connections — verify clean drain without orphaned connections
  - Race between health check failure and acquire — verify correct fallback to new connection creation

## Benchmark requirements
- **Criterion benchmarks for pool scaling with connection count**: Add `benches/mux_pool_scaling.rs` measuring:
  - Acquire + release cycle latency as a function of pool size (max_size=1,2,4,8,16,32)
  - Health check overhead per acquire operation
  - Pool scaling: throughput (ops/sec) with increasing concurrent task count
  - Idle eviction scan time as a function of pool size
  - Connection factory overhead (DirectMuxClient::connect latency)","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T03:50:21.992539Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-10T19:49:43.764726Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-2h5wv","depends_on_id":"wa-36idf","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T03:51:59.989246Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"},{"issue_id":"wa-2h5wv","depends_on_id":"wa-p48pw","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T03:52:00.095469Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"}]}

+### Async Runtime: asupersync
 
-This project uses **tokio** for async operations. The `frankenterm` CLI binary and `frankenterm-core` library use tokio for I/O, timers, and task spawning.
+This project must use **asupersync** for async operations. The intended runtime model for the `frankenterm` CLI binary and `frankenterm-core` library is `Cx`-aware, structured, cancel-correct async built around asupersync.
 
-**Note:** Some optional features (MCP server mode) bring in `asupersync` and `fastmcp` as dependencies for MCP protocol support, but the primary runtime is tokio.
+**Policy:** direct `tokio` usage is forbidden. Any remaining `tokio` references in manifests, `runtime_compat`, tests, benches, or comments are migration debt to remove, not an accepted runtime choice.
 
 ### Key Dependencies
 
 | Crate | Purpose |

-| `tokio` | Async runtime (full features) |

 | `toon_rust` | Token-Optimized Object Notation (AI-to-AI format) |
 | `clap` | CLI argument parsing |
